content.text:
# Why Planets Are Round (Not Lumpy!)

Great question! Let me explain it simply. 🌍

## It's All About Gravity!

Imagine you're rolling a big ball of Play-Doh in your hands. Gravity does something similar to planets, but with a super-strong pull!

**Here's the trick:** Gravity always pulls toward the center of an object. So every single part of a planet—every rock, every mountain—gets pulled toward the middle. This pulling from *all directions equally* squishes everything into a ball shape!

## Why Not Lumpy?

When a planet is **really, really big**, gravity is super strong. If a giant mountain tried to stick up too high...

- Gravity would pull it back down
- The rock isn't strong enough to hold up against that mighty pull
- So it flattens out!

Think of it like this: 🏔️ A tiny ant can stand on your hand easily, but a heavy elephant would squish you flat. Big planets are so heavy that gravity "squishes" any big lumps down.

## So When DO We See Lumpy Space Rocks?

We actually DO see lumpy things in space! 🪨

- **Small asteroids** and **little space rocks** are often lumpy and weird-shaped
- That's because they're **too small** for gravity to be strong enough to round them out!

## The Simple Rule:

| Size | Shape |
|------|-------|
| **Big** (like Earth) | Round ⚪ |
| **Small** (like asteroids) | Lumpy 🥔 |

So planets are round because they're big enough that gravity wins and pulls everything into a nice ball!

Want me to explain anything else? 😊
